tracker_summary.invention: Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) — inert, nearly frictionless, chemically resistant polymer
tracker_summary.date: Discovered April 6, 1938; patented 1941; trade-named 'Teflon' 1944; first consumer cookware (Tefal) 1956
tracker_summary.current_use: Nonstick cookware; aerospace seals; medical implants; semiconductor manufacturing; Gore-Tex
tracker_summary.initial_funding_source: DuPont / Kinetic Chemicals Inc. (DuPont-GM joint venture)
tracker_summary.initial_funding_reason: Developing safer, non-toxic refrigerant gases to replace ammonia and sulfur dioxide

the_x: DuPont's refrigerant R&D program searching for safer alternatives to toxic industrial refrigerants
the_y: PTFE (Teflon) — one of the most chemically inert substances ever created, from nonstick cookware to spacecraft
summary: DuPont chemist Roy Plunkett was working on refrigerant gases in 1938 when he checked a cylinder of tetrafluoroethylene gas that registered empty on the pressure gauge but still had the correct weight. Instead of discarding it, he cut it open and found a slippery white powder — the gas had spontaneously polymerized into PTFE. The material was so inert it was immediately classified and used in the Manhattan Project to contain corrosive uranium hexafluoride. After the war, a French engineer's wife suggested he coat cooking pans with it, launching the Tefal company.

chain.who: Dr. Roy J. Plunkett and Jack Rebok
chain.where: DuPont Jackson Laboratory, Deepwater, New Jersey
chain.what_they_were_doing: Preparing TFE gas for refrigerant synthesis; a stored cylinder showed empty pressure but correct weight.
chain.the_jump: They cut it open with a hacksaw. Inside: a white, waxy, slippery powder — TFE had spontaneously polymerized into PTFE. Extraordinarily inert, heat-stable, frictionless.

chain.who: Manhattan Project / U.S. Army Corps of Engineers
chain.what_they_were_doing: Used PTFE (classified 'K-416') to line valves and seals in uranium enrichment equipment.
chain.the_jump: Large-scale PTFE manufacturing built for the bomb established the industrial processes needed for post-war commercialization.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Declassified PTFE; trademarked it 'Teflon'; began high-priced industrial sales.

chain.who: Marc Grégoire and his wife Colette
chain.what_they_were_doing: Grégoire coated fishing tackle with Teflon; Colette suggested applying it to cooking pans.
chain.the_jump: After two years solving the adhesion problem, Grégoire founded Tefal in 1956 — selling the first nonstick frying pans to immediate consumer success.
chain.funding: Self-funded; later private investment in Tefal.

chain.what_they_were_doing: PTFE expanded into aerospace heat shields, joint replacements, Gore-Tex (1969), semiconductor manufacturing.

sources: Science History Institute. 'Roy Plunkett and the Discovery of Teflon.' https://www.sciencehistory.org/historical-profile/roy-j-plunkett
ACS. 'Discovery of Teflon — National Historic Chemical Landmark.' 2014. https://www.acs.org/education/whatischemistry/landmarks/teflon.html
Rhodes R. 'The Making of the Atomic Bomb.' Simon & Schuster, 1986.
